title: The Mental Load
subtitle: A reading on the invisible work of running a household, why it sits heavy, and what to do about it
excerpt: A twenty-minute reading on the mental load: the planning, the noticing, the remembering, the running of the household in your head. Named by sociologists in the 1970s and made famous again by Emma Clit's 2017 comic. The work is real, even when no one else can see it.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The mental-load frame draws on Allison Daminger's work on cognitive labour (Harvard, 2019), Emma Clit's 2017 comic 'You Should've Asked', and the earlier sociology on household labour.

practice.whenToUse: Read when the running of the household is sitting heavy on you and no one in the home can see the work. Also good before a household conversation about who does what.

content.text: There is a kind of work that does not show up on any to-do list. The remembering of when the school shoes will be too small. The knowing that the dog is due a worm tablet. The keeping in mind that the parents are coming on Saturday and there is nothing in for lunch. This work has a name. It is the mental load.

The mental load is the planning and the noticing and the remembering that runs a household. It is the work the body does in the background while it is also doing the visible work. Most of the time, in most households, it is carried by one person.

The sociologist Allison Daminger broke the mental load into four parts. Anticipating what needs to happen. Identifying the options. Deciding which option to take. Monitoring whether it happened. Each part is invisible. Each part takes mental room.

When one partner does most of these four parts, the other partner can do a lot of the visible work and still leave the planner with all the mental load. The list of what was done is not the list of what was carried.

The mental load is heavy because it never closes. The to-do list can be finished. The mental load runs in the background of every conversation, every meal, every car journey. There is no point in the day when it is fully off.
It is also heavy because it is rarely seen. The person carrying it knows what they are carrying. The other people in the house often do not.
What helps in the short term
Get the list out of your head. A shared family list, a written weekly plan, anything that means the planning is not all in one head.
Hand over whole areas, not single tasks. "You own the school admin" is workable. "Can you remember to sign the form" leaves the planning with you.
Let the other person do it their way, even if it would not be your way. The transfer fails when you keep checking.
What helps in the long term

Talking about the mental load directly, with the people in the household, is the long-term move. The talk is not a one-off. It is a few short talks over months, not one big one. The aim is a shared picture of what the work actually is.
Be patient. The mental load does not redistribute in a week. It does redistribute over months when the talks keep happening.

Original to homemade.education. The mental-load frame draws on Allison Daminger's work on cognitive labour at Harvard in 2019. It also draws on Emma Clit's 2017 comic 'You Should've Asked' and on earlier sociology on household labour.
